comments.author: @khecke
comments.text: I was born in Germany by the end of 1933 and lived the first 23 years of my life in Germany. Germany has changed a lot since that time.

comments.author: @tonyfernandes3557
comments.text: "Made in Germany" used to be but it's not anymore! Now, besides being square Germans are the most Americanized people in Europe! Without the cheap Russian energy and resources I'm curious about Germany's future!

comments.author: @NICEGLOBE05
comments.text: Brilliant editing and storytelling! The transition from the chaotic, industrial techno scene of Berlin to the absolute, pristine silence of the Black Forest was phenomenal. Germany is so much more than just efficient factories and fast cars on the Autobahn. 10/10

comments.author: @ManoSaunders
comments.text: Just happy I am watching from Cape Town South Africa just don't understand because it's too much but beautiful good day

comments.author: @GlobalCountryExplorer
comments.text: Germany always surprises me. Beyond the beer and festivals, there’s such a profound sense of history and discipline. It’s a complex, fascinating culture that feels both modern and deeply rooted in tradition.

replies.author: @tonyfernandes3557
replies.text: Sense of history?... Except for the periods during which they killed millions of people!

comments.author: @James-j4k5j
comments.text: I'm watching from Alabama, however I was born in Wiesbaden, Germany when my father was stationed there by the U.S. Air Force. Also, there is a speed limit on the autobahn, a minimum speed limit!

comments.author: @GlobalRealLifeDocumentary
comments.text: Germany is one of those countries that keeps surprising me—the more I learn about its history, culture, engineering, and stunning landscapes, the more I realize how much there is to explore. 🇩🇪 After watching this documentary, what changed your opinion about Germany the most?
